title: ელექტრონული დოკუმენტისა და ელექტრონული სანდო მომსახურების შესახებ საქართველოს კანონი
title_en: Law of Georgia on Electronic Document and Electronic Trust Services
description: The Law of Georgia on Electronic Document and Electronic Trust Services establishes the legal framework for electronic signatures, electronic seals, electronic time stamps, and other trust services in Georgia. The law is aligned with the EU eIDAS Regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014) as part of Georgia's EU approximation commitments under the Association Agreement. The Georgian Accreditation Center (GAC) under the Digital Governance Agency acts as the supervisory body for trust service providers.

provisions.title: Article 1. Purpose and Scope
provisions.content: The purpose of this Law is to establish the legal framework for electronic documents and electronic trust services in Georgia, to enable secure electronic interactions between citizens, businesses, and government, and to facilitate the use of electronic signatures and other trust services in legal, commercial, and administrative transactions. This Law shall apply to: (a) trust service providers established in Georgia; (b) electronic signatures and other trust services used in Georgia; and (c) electronic documents created, transmitted, and stored in Georgia. The Law is aligned with the EU Regulation on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 - eIDAS) and aims to ensure interoperability with EU trust services, as part of Georgia's commitments under the EU-Georgia Association Agreement. Reference: Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS); Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS2).

provisions.title: Article 2. Definitions
provisions.content: For the purposes of this Law: 'Electronic signature' means data in electronic form which is attached to or logically associated with other data in electronic form and which is used by the signatory to sign. 'Qualified electronic signature' means an advanced electronic signature that is created by a qualified electronic signature creation device and which is based on a qualified certificate for electronic signatures. 'Advanced electronic signature' means an electronic signature which is uniquely linked to the signatory, capable of identifying the signatory, created using electronic signature creation data that the signatory can, with a high level of confidence, use under his sole control, and linked to the data signed therewith in such a way that any subsequent change in the data is detectable. 'Trust service' means an electronic service normally provided for remuneration which consists of the creation, verification, validation, electronic signing, electronic sealing, electronic time stamping, registered electronic delivery, and website authentication services. 'Trust service provider' means a natural or legal person who provides one or more trust services. 'Qualified trust service provider' means a trust service provider who provides one or more qualified trust services and is granted the qualified status by the supervisory body.

provisions.title: Article 3. Legal Effect of Electronic Signatures
provisions.content: An electronic signature shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an electronic form or that it does not meet the requirements for qualified electronic signatures. A qualified electronic signature shall have the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature. Where a law or regulation requires a handwritten signature, that requirement is met by a qualified electronic signature. Electronic signatures shall be presumed to be legally equivalent to handwritten signatures in any legal, commercial, or administrative transaction, unless the applicable law requires a handwritten signature or a specific form of authentication. A qualified electronic signature based on a qualified certificate issued in Georgia shall be recognised as a qualified electronic signature, and where compatible, as equivalent to qualified electronic signatures issued by trust service providers in EU member states, in accordance with interoperability arrangements under the Association Agreement.

provisions.title: Article 4. Electronic Documents
provisions.content: An electronic document shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in electronic form. An electronic document signed with a qualified electronic signature shall have the same legal effect as a document signed with a handwritten signature, provided that: (a) the electronic document can be accessed and read throughout its required retention period; (b) the integrity of the document is maintained; and (c) the authorship and time of signing are verifiable. Electronic documents submitted to public authorities shall be accepted in lieu of paper documents, unless the relevant law specifically requires a paper document. Public authorities shall, in their electronic communications and document management, use certified electronic document management systems meeting the requirements established by the Digital Governance Agency.

provisions.title: Article 5. Supervisory Body and Trust Service List
provisions.content: The Georgian Accreditation Center (GAC) under the Digital Governance Agency shall be the supervisory body for trust service providers. The GAC shall: (a) maintain and publish the Georgian Trusted List of qualified trust service providers and their qualified trust services; (b) supervise compliance of trust service providers with this Law; (c) investigate complaints about trust service providers; (d) impose sanctions on trust service providers that violate this Law; and (e) cooperate with supervisory bodies of EU member states on trust service matters. The Georgian Trusted List shall be maintained and published in a standardized format compatible with EU trust lists. Trust service providers shall be listed in the Georgian Trusted List only after the GAC has verified that they meet the requirements of this Law. The Georgian Trusted List shall be available free of charge and in machine-readable format.

provisions.title: Article 6. Qualified Trust Service Providers
provisions.content: Trust service providers wishing to obtain qualified status shall apply to the GAC. A trust service provider may be granted qualified status where it demonstrates that it: (a) provides trust services that comply with the technical requirements established under this Law; (b) has implemented appropriate technical and organisational security measures; (c) employs qualified and experienced staff; (d) maintains adequate financial resources or insurance to cover liability; and (e) has undergone a conformity assessment by an accredited conformity assessment body. The GAC shall notify the applicant of its decision within 3 months of the receipt of a complete application. Qualified status shall be indicated by a qualified trust service seal on all qualified trust services provided. Qualified trust service providers shall undergo conformity assessment at least every 24 months and shall inform the GAC of any changes to their services.

provisions.title: Article 7. Electronic Seals
provisions.content: An electronic seal shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an electronic form or that it does not meet the requirements for qualified electronic seals. A qualified electronic seal shall enjoy a presumption of integrity of the data and of the correctness of the origin of that data to which the qualified electronic seal is linked. A qualified electronic seal shall have equivalent legal effect to a document bearing an official stamp, where Georgian law requires such a stamp. Legal entities, including public bodies, may use electronic seals to authenticate documents they issue. Public authorities shall accept qualified electronic seals from legal entities as equivalent to official stamps in administrative proceedings. Electronic seals shall be used by public authorities in the automated processing of decisions.

provisions.title: Article 8. Electronic Time Stamps
provisions.content: An electronic time stamp shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an electronic form or does not meet the requirements for qualified electronic time stamps. A qualified electronic time stamp shall enjoy a presumption of the accuracy of the date and time it indicates and the integrity of the data to which the date and time are bound. Electronic time stamping services are essential for establishing the timing of electronic signatures and documents, for maintaining audit trails, and for establishing the existence of electronic documents at a particular time. Qualified time stamps issued by qualified trust service providers in Georgia shall be recognized in legal proceedings as evidence of the date and time of electronic events.

provisions.title: Article 9. Electronic Identity and Authentication
provisions.content: The Digital Governance Agency shall establish and operate a national electronic identity scheme enabling Georgian citizens and legal entities to authenticate themselves electronically in interactions with public authorities and, where private sector entities accept such means, in private sector transactions. The national electronic identity scheme shall: (a) provide at minimum medium-level and high-level assurance as defined in alignment with EU standards; (b) be accessible to all Georgian citizens and residents; (c) be interoperable with EU electronic identity schemes, in accordance with interoperability arrangements under the Association Agreement and the eIDAS regulation; and (d) be compatible with the European Digital Identity Wallet framework (eIDAS2). Public authorities shall accept electronic identification means of sufficient assurance level in all their electronic services.

provisions.title: Article 10. Security Requirements for Trust Services
provisions.content: Trust service providers shall take appropriate technical and organisational measures to manage the security risks posed to the trust services they provide. These measures shall, taking into account the latest developments, ensure that the level of security of the trust service is appropriate to the degree of risk. In particular, trust service providers shall: (a) take precautions against and limit consequences of security incidents; (b) prevent and minimize the impact of security incidents; (c) report significant security incidents to the GAC within 24 hours; (d) where the incident has an impact on natural persons to whom the service has been provided, notify those persons without undue delay; and (e) preserve data logs and audit trails of their trust services for the period required by the GAC. The GAC shall issue technical requirements for security measures, aligned with ENISA guidelines.

provisions.title: Article 11. Cross-Border Recognition
provisions.content: Trust services provided by qualified trust service providers established in Georgia shall be recognized by public authorities in EU member states in accordance with interoperability arrangements established under the EU-Georgia Association Agreement. Qualified electronic signatures and qualified electronic seals issued by Georgian qualified trust service providers shall be recognized in EU member states to the extent provided for in such arrangements. The GAC shall enter into mutual recognition arrangements with supervisory bodies in EU member states for the recognition of trust services. Where no mutual recognition arrangement exists, parties to cross-border transactions may use contractual mechanisms to recognize foreign trust services. The Digital Governance Agency shall promote the development of interoperability between Georgian and EU trust service infrastructures. Reference: Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 14 on international trust services.

provisions.title: Article 12. Liability of Trust Service Providers
provisions.content: Trust service providers shall be liable to any natural or legal person for damages caused intentionally or negligently to any natural or legal person due to a failure to comply with the obligations under this Law. A qualified trust service provider shall be liable for damages caused to any natural or legal person due to: (a) inaccuracy of information contained in the qualified certificate at the time of issuance; (b) failure to meet requirements for qualified certificates; (c) failure to register the revocation of qualified certificates; (d) failure to meet the applicable security requirements. The burden of proof of intent or negligence shall be on the trust service provider. A trust service provider may limit its liability in the certificate, provided such limitation is communicated to users prior to the conclusion of the service agreement. Trust service providers shall maintain adequate professional liability insurance or provide alternative financial security.

provisions.title: Article 13. Preservation of Electronic Documents
provisions.content: Where Georgian law requires the preservation of documents for a specified period, electronic documents shall satisfy such requirements where: (a) the information is accessible throughout the required preservation period; (b) the electronic document is preserved in the format in which it was created, sent or received, or in a format which can be demonstrated to accurately represent the information originally generated, sent or received; (c) the information enables identification of the origin and destination of the document, and the date and time when it was sent or received; and (d) the integrity of the document is maintained throughout the preservation period. The Digital Governance Agency shall issue standards for the long-term preservation of electronic documents, taking into account the risk of technological obsolescence and the need for secure long-term storage of cryptographic evidence.

provisions.title: Article 14. Government Use of Trust Services
provisions.content: Public authorities in Georgia shall use qualified electronic signatures and qualified electronic seals in all their electronic transactions requiring authentication or integrity assurance, as soon as technically feasible. Public authorities shall use qualified electronic signatures from nationally accredited trust service providers in official communications with citizens and legal entities. The Government of Georgia shall develop and implement a national e-government framework based on trust services, enabling end-to-end electronic processing of administrative procedures. All government portals and digital services shall accept qualified electronic signatures and shall provide mechanisms for their use that are accessible to persons with disabilities.

provisions.title: Article 15. Administrative Sanctions
provisions.content: The GAC may impose administrative sanctions on trust service providers that violate this Law. Administrative sanctions shall include: (a) a warning, with a requirement to remedy the violation within a specified period; (b) administrative fines: up to GEL 100,000 for qualified trust service providers and up to GEL 50,000 for non-qualified trust service providers; (c) suspension of the trust service; (d) removal from the Georgian Trusted List. Decisions of the GAC shall be subject to judicial review. Fines shall be proportionate to the gravity of the violation, the harm caused, and the financial capacity of the trust service provider. The GAC shall publish its decisions on sanctions on its website, with appropriate protection for commercially sensitive information, to maintain public confidence in the trust service ecosystem.

definitions.definition: An advanced electronic signature that is created by a qualified electronic signature creation device and which is based on a qualified certificate for electronic signatures.

An electronic service normally provided for remuneration which consists of the creation, verification, validation, electronic signing, electronic sealing, electronic time stamping, registered electronic delivery, and website authentication services.
A trust service provider who provides one or more qualified trust services and is granted the qualified status by the supervisory body (Georgian Accreditation Center).
